text: We present a video on the usefulness of S and H pouch configurations in redo pouch surgery for failed ileal pouches. The S pouch. The S pouch can reach up to two to four centimeters further into the pelvis compared with the J pouch. So it is usually created if there is excessive tension on the IPAA.

The surgeon constructs an S pouch using three limbs of terminal ileum, each measuring twelve to fifteen centimeters. A two-centimeter exit conduit is formed, and the ileal segments are approximated with continuous seromuscular sutures. The enterotomy line is marked in an S-shaped configuration using a bovie.
The surgeon places stay sutures to maintain the proper orientation of the S pouch, ensuring the sutures are positioned close to the mesentery on the two outer limbs to maximize the reservoir size.
The surgeon uses a tonsil or gloved finger to assist in making linear enterotomies on the bowel, following the pre-marked seromuscular bovie lines to maintain orientation during the division process.

The surgeon uses 3-0 braided absorbable sutures to perform a continuous running suture with full thickness bites along the two posterior anastomotic lines of the S-pouch. The anterior wall is closed with continuous seromuscular sutures, ensuring the lateral wings of the S-pouch come together without tension.
The surgeon performs an insufflation test using normal saline to check the integrity of the staple lines, ensuring the inlet of the S-pouch is not narrowed.

The surgeon opens the apex of the proposed J pouch with sharp dissection to extend the afferent limb further into the pelvis. The distal portion of the ileum, intended as the efferent limb, is stapled at both ends and the staple lines are reinforced with continuous three-O absorbable sutures. This isolated ileal segment remains in mesenteric continuity with the afferent limb and is aligned alongside it in an isoperistaltic orientation.

The surgeon creates an enterotomy at the halfway point of each limb to form the H pouch. Two linear staple fires are applied, one directed cephalad and the other caudal, through the previously made enterotomy.

The surgeon ensures the staple line is on the anti-mesenteric border of the bowel to prevent bleeding. The right limb of the H pouch is stapled off using a TA stapler, and firing the stapler in a caudal direction creates a common channel with one lumen for the iliopouch anal anastomosis.

The surgeon places two stay sutures at the corners of the common enterotomy. The common enterotomy is then closed using a continuous running seromuscular suture with 3-0 absorbable braided material. An insufflation test is performed to verify the integrity of the staple lines.

In conclusion, although a J pouch is most commonly constructed, an S or H pouch may be necessary for some patients to avoid a permanent stoma, as sometimes a J pouch is met with anatomic or technical difficulties. S and H pouch construction must be in the armamentarium of anyone performing index or redo pouch procedures.
